title: Math — Two-Variable Data
description: SAT-style two-variable data questions: lines of best fit, slope, residuals, and interpreting linear models from scatterplots.

questions.prompt:
The scatterplot shows the relationship between two variables, \(x\) and \(y\). A line of best fit is also shown.

Which of the following is closest to the slope of the line of best fit shown?

solution.approach: Estimate two points that lie on the line of best fit and compute slope \(m=\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\).
steps: The line of best fit slopes downward as \(x\) increases, so the slope must be negative (eliminate positive choices).
Pick two clear points the line passes near, for example \((1,12)\) and \((11,4)\).
Compute the slope: \(m=\frac{4-12}{11-1}=\frac{-8}{10}=-0.8\).
So the closest choice is \(-0.8\).
solution.takeaway: For best-fit slope questions, (1) decide the sign from the trend, then (2) estimate slope using two easy points on the line.

questions.prompt:
The scatterplot shows the relationship between two variables, \(x\) and \(y\). A line of best fit is also shown.

Which of the following is closest to the slope of this line of best fit?

solution.approach: Use two approximate points on the best-fit line and compute \(m=\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\).
steps: The line decreases as \(x\) increases, so the slope is negative (eliminate the positive choices).
Use two clear points on the line, about \((0,14)\) and \((13,0)\).
Compute slope: \(m=\frac{0-14}{13-0}=\frac{-14}{13}\approx -1.08\).
The closest choice is \(-1.1\).
solution.takeaway: When the fraction isn’t clean, estimate the decimal and choose the closest option.

questions.prompt:
During a study, the temperature, in degrees Celsius (\(^\circ\text{C}\)), of the air in a chamber was recorded at certain times.

What was the average rate of change, in \(^\circ\text{C}\) per minute, of the recorded temperature from \(x=5\) to \(x=7\)?

solution.approach: Average rate of change is slope between the two points: \(\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\).
steps: Read the y-value at \(x=5\): the plot shows \(y=14\).
Read the y-value at \(x=7\): the plot shows \(y=24\).
Compute average rate of change: \(\frac{24-14}{7-5}=\frac{10}{2}=5\).
solution.takeaway: Average rate of change between two times is the slope between those two plotted points.

questions.prompt:
The scatterplot shows the federal-mandated minimum wage every 10 years between 1940 and 2010. A line of best fit is shown, and its equation is

\[y=0.096x-0.488\]

What does the line of best fit predict about the increase in the minimum wage over the 70-year period?
choices: Each year between 1940 and 2010, the average increase in minimum wage was 0.096 dollars.
Each year between 1940 and 2010, the average increase in minimum wage was 0.49 dollars.
Every 10 years between 1940 and 2010, the average increase in minimum wage was 0.096 dollars.
Every 10 years between 1940 and 2010, the average increase in minimum wage was 0.488 dollars.

solution.finalAnswer: Each year between 1940 and 2010, the average increase in minimum wage was 0.096 dollars.
solution.approach: In \(y=mx+b\), the slope \(m\) is the change in \(y\) for a 1-unit increase in \(x\).
steps: The equation is in slope-intercept form \(y=mx+b\). Here, \(m=0.096\).
The x-axis is labeled as years since 1940, so 1 unit of \(x\) = 1 year.
Therefore, the model predicts the minimum wage increases by \(0.096\) dollars per year on average.
That matches choice A.
solution.takeaway: Always match the slope’s “per 1 x-unit” meaning to the axis units (years, decades, minutes, etc.).

questions.prompt: Which of the following could be the equation for a line of best fit for the data shown in the scatterplot?

solution.approach: Decide the sign of the slope from the trend, then estimate the slope magnitude and intercept from representative points.
steps: The data increase as \(x\) increases, so the slope must be positive (eliminate choices with negative slope).
Estimate two points near the overall trend, e.g., about \((1,4)\) and \((8,26)\).
Estimated slope: \(m\approx\frac{26-4}{8-1}=\frac{22}{7}\approx3.14\), which is closest to 3 (not 0.8).
Extrapolating back toward \(x=0\), the line would cross near \(y\approx1\), consistent with \(+0.8\).
So \(y=3x+0.8\) is a reasonable best-fit equation.
solution.takeaway: A best-fit line needs the right slope direction (sign) and the right steepness (magnitude) more than “perfectly hitting” points.

questions.prompt:
The scatterplot shows the relationship between two variables, \(x\) and \(y\). A line of best fit is also shown.

For how many of the 10 data points is the actual \(y\)-value greater than the \(y\)-value predicted by the line of best fit?

solution.approach: Points above the line have actual \(y\) greater than predicted \(y\). Count those points.
steps: A point above the best-fit line means actual \(y\) is greater than predicted \(y\).
Scan the graph and count how many dots lie above the line of best fit.
There are 6 points above the line.
Therefore, the answer is 6.
solution.takeaway: Residual sign: above line = positive residual (actual > predicted); below line = negative residual (actual < predicted).

questions.prompt:
The scatterplot shows the relationship between two variables, \(x\) and \(y\).

Which of the following equations is the most appropriate linear model for the data shown?

solution.approach: Choose the model whose slope sign matches the trend and whose intercept matches the y-value near \(x=0\).
steps: As \(x\) increases, the points generally go down, so the slope must be negative (eliminate choices with positive slope).
Near \(x=0\), the y-values are around 9–10, so the y-intercept should be around 9–10 (not near 1).
Among the negative-slope options, \(y=9.4-0.9x\) has y-intercept 9.4 and slope -0.9, matching both requirements.
Therefore, choice D is best.
solution.takeaway: Model selection = match the direction (slope sign) and the starting level (y-intercept) to the scatterplot.

questions.prompt:
In the given scatterplot, a line of best fit for the data is shown.

Which of the following is closest to the slope of the line of best fit shown?

solution.approach: Estimate two points on the line of best fit and compute \(m=\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\).
steps: Pick two easy points the best-fit line passes near, such as \((0,0.2)\) and \((5,9.3)\).
The closest choice to 1.82 is 1.8.
solution.takeaway: When answer choices are decimals, compute a decimal slope and then select the closest option.
